stem: A sphere's radius grows at \( \frac{dr}{dt}=[[drdt]] \). When \( r=[[r]] \), how fast is the volume changing (\( \frac{dV}{dt}=4\pi r^{2}\frac{dr}{dt} \))?
A cube's edge length grows at \( \frac{ds}{dt}=[[dsdt]] \). When \( s=[[s]] \), how fast is the volume changing (\( V=s^{3} \), so \( \frac{dV}{dt}=3s^{2}\frac{ds}{dt} \))?
distractor_rationales: This drops the square on \( s \).
This is the volume itself, not its rate of change.
This drops the factor of 3 from the power rule.
This uses the exponent as if \( V=s^{2}\), not \( s^{3} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A tank shaped like an inverted cone is filled with water so that the surface radius is always \( [[k]] \) times the depth \( h \) (that is, \( r=[[k]]h \)), giving volume \( V=\dfrac{\pi}{3}([[k]]h)^{2}h \). If the depth is rising at \( \frac{dh}{dt}=[[dhdt]] \), how fast is the volume changing when \( h=[[h]] \) (i.e. \( \frac{dV}{dt}=\pi [[k]]^{2}h^{2}\frac{dh}{dt} \))?
solution: Substituting \( r=[[k]]h \) into \( V=\frac{\pi}{3}r^{2}h \) gives \( V=\frac{\pi}{3}[[k]]^{2}h^{3} \), so \( \frac{dV}{dt}=\pi [[k]]^{2}h^{2}\frac{dh}{dt}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the ratio \( k \).
This forgets to square the depth \( h \).
This uses \( V \) itself instead of differentiating to get \( \frac{dV}{dt}\).
This has an extra factor of 2 from a chain-rule slip.
This adds an extraneous \( \pi \) term.
This doubles the correct rate.

stem: Water is pumped into a cylindrical tank of fixed radius \( [[r]] \) at a rate \( \frac{dV}{dt}=[[dVdt]] \) (volume per unit time). Since \( V=\pi r^{2}h \) with \( r \) constant, how fast is the water level \( h \) rising (find \( \frac{dh}{dt} \))?
solution: Since \( \frac{dV}{dt}=\pi r^{2}\frac{dh}{dt} \) with \( r \) fixed, \( \frac{dh}{dt}=\dfrac{dV/dt}{\pi r^{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \( \pi \) in the denominator.
This uses the circle's circumference formula instead of its area.
This multiplies by \( \pi r^{2} \) instead of dividing.
This forgets to square the radius.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A spherical balloon's radius grows at \( \frac{dr}{dt}=[[drdt]] \). When \( r=[[r]] \), how fast is its SURFACE AREA changing? (Recall \( A=4\pi r^{2} \), so \( \frac{dA}{dt}=8\pi r\frac{dr}{dt} \).)
distractor_rationales: This uses the coefficient from the area formula itself, without differentiating.
This forgets to reduce the power of \( r \) from 2 to 1.
This is the surface area itself, not its rate of change.
This drops the factor of \( r \).
This adds an extraneous \( \pi \) term.
This doubles the correct rate.
